title: Abstract
content: Selective photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 into methanol, using 355 nm UV laser as a light source, has been studied for the first time.An alternate route for the photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 , based on thermodynamic viability of the process, has been suggested.It was observed that the formation of methanol from CO 2 is a reversible process and the methanol produced during the photocatalytic process degrades to generate hydrogen and CO 2 again; which hampers the over all conversion rate.The measurements on pH and hydrogen production during the course of photocatalytic reduction were valuable in explaining the conversion process.

title: Experimental
content: The photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 into methanol over TiO 2 , NiO, and ZnO was studied by irradiating the saturated solution of CO 2 in 70 ml of deionized water containing a suspension of the optimum amount of the above-mentioned semiconductor powders.A Pyrex cell (35 mm diameter and 120 mm length), equipped with optical grade quartz windows for transmission of UV laser beam was designed and fabricated.The cell was equipped with ports and rubber septa for liquid and gas sampling.In order to study the effect of different parameters on methanol yield, the first step was to optimize the amount of photocatalyst and laser energy for maximum production of methanol.Between 50 and 1000 mg was used for the optimization of the amount of catalyst while for the laser energy, a range of 20-200 mJ per pulse was used.The detailed procedure employed for the optimization of above-mentioned parameters is discussed in detail elsewhere 11121314.In order to study the photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 into methanol, the optimum amount of photocatalysts, ( 300 mg) was suspended in 70 ml of doubly distilled deionized water.Before irradiation, the colloidal suspension was saturated with CO 2 by passing the CO 2 through the suspension at a flow rate of 150 ml min for a period of 15 min.The suspension was then irradiated for 90 min with the optimum laser energy of 355 nm pulsed laser.A 355 nm laser beam, was generated from the third harmonic of a Spectra Physics Nd:YAG laser (Model GCR 250).The pulse width of the laser was 8 ns with a 10 Hz repetition rate.The laser beam was directed into the center of the reaction chamber using a set of mirrors and collimators.For all the experiments, the laser beam diameter was kept constant at 10 mm. The gases that formed were analyzed by removing 100 ll of gas sample from the dead volume of the photocatalytic reactor with a gas-tight syringe at regular time intervals.The samples were analyzed by using gas chromatograph (GC) (Shimadzu, Model GC-17) equipped with 30 m molecular sieve 5A plot column and a TCD detector. The liquid products were analyzed with the GC mentioned above.A 30 m, 0.53 mm diameter, RT-Q plot column and FID were used.Helium was used as the carrier gas.All the experiments were performed at room temperature and atmospheric pressure.

title: Results and discussion
content: To evaluate the process of photoreduction of CO 2 to methanol in aqueous medium, it is important to consider the basic processes occurring at the surface of the photocatalyst after absorption of the photons of energy equal to, or greater than, the bandgap.The bandgap excitation leads to the formation of conduction band electrons and valance band holes, which serve as the sites for photoreduction and photooxidation, respectively.The photooxidation of water, the solvent, leads to the formation of hydroxyl radicals (OH A ) and H ions through water oxidation by the valance band holes produced due to laser irradiation of the semiconductor catalyst 151617181920.The hydroxyl radicals generate oxygen while H ions form hydrogen by capturing conduction band electrons; i.e., SC When CO 2 is present in the system, a part of it remains in the free form as CO 2 while a considerable portion is dissolved in the water to form H 2 CO 3 as given below. The dissociation of H 2 CO 3 is of acidic nature and generates H and carbonate ions. The comparison of the amount of methanol produced from the photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 , over TiO 2 , NiO, and ZnO, as a function of time, is presented in Fig. 1.It is observed that for TiO 2 and ZnO there is an increase followed by a sharp decrease in the yield of methanol while for NiO there is an initial sharp increase followed by a gradual decrease.Other side products observed in the process were CH 3 COOH (in the liquid phase) and methane and CO (in the gas phase).Although the side products were identified, the exact quantification of these products was not possible due to the low concentration and the sensitivity limits of our GC. CO 2 is, thermodynamically, a very stable molecule and difficult to reduce or oxidize.The mechanism of methanol production from photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 is quite complex and most of the researchers agree that this process is multi-electron transfer instead of single electron transfer as the electrochemical potential of A2.0 V for a single electron process (for the conversion of CO 2 -CH 3 OH) does not support it 21,22.The possible reactions that can occur in the reduction of CO 2 in the aqueous medium, under bandgap illumination, ranging from one to eight electrons transfer, with thermodynamic potentials versus normal hydrogen electrode (NHE), are mentioned below 21,22. It is clear from Eqs. ( 7)-( 12) that the multi-electronic processes (Eqs.( 8)-( 12)) are more favorable; they require much less energy per electron transferred as compared to mono-electron process (Eq.7).A simple tool, in photocatalytic processes, to study the feasibility of a reaction occurring is the position of the conduction and valance band edges.These band edges predict directly the occurrence of a reaction.The conduction band potentials of the catalysts (TiO 2 , NiO, and ZnO) used in this study are A0.29,A0.5 and A0.31 V, respectively, at pH 7 23.By comparing the conduction band edge potentials of the catalysts used with that of the thermodynamic potentials of the reactions (Eqs.( 7)-( 12)), it can be predicted that the reactions in Eqs. ( 11) and ( 12) can lead to the desired products.The formation of H 2 CO 3 , with the dissolution of CO 2 , decreases the pH of the system.This shifts the potential of the conduction band edge to more positive value, which further reduces the probability of the formation of methanol through the reaction in Eq. (11).A comparison of the pH changes in the presence of argon as well as CO 2 under UV laser illumination is presented in Fig. 2. Here, a decrease in the pH of the system, in the presence of CO 2 compared to that without CO 2 , can be observed even without illumination.This observation strongly supports the formation of H 2 CO 3 in the system.Secondly, the above-mentioned reactions are only possible with the free CO 2 in the solution.The H 2 CO 3 can be reduced to a number of products such as CH 3 OH, HCOOH, and HCHO through multi-electron transfer processes 21,22: Similarly, the carbonate ions present in the solution can lead to a number of products through multi-electron transfer processes: The molecular species and anions produced because of the reduction of H 2 CO 3 (Eqs.( 13)-( 15)) and carbonate ions (Eqs.( 16)-( 18)), serve as precursors for the additional formation of methanol. By comparing, the potentials of the reactions given in Eqs. ( 13)- (20) with that of the conduction band potentials of the catalysts under study, it can be inferred that the route of methanol formation in Eqs. ( 13)-( 20) is more thermodynamically favorable compared to the route in Eqs. ( 7)- (12).As was stated earlier, in Fig. 1, it can be observed that for TiO 2 and ZnO, the yield of methanol decreases sharply with time while for NiO, although there is an increase in the yield of methanol, the rate of methanol formation decreases with time.As all the reactions were performed at room temperature, the methanol that is produced by the photocatalytic reduction of CO 2 remains in the solution and when its concentration reaches an appreciable value, it starts competing with water for valence band holes.The transfer of electrons to holes leads to the transformation of methanol to other compounds such as formaldehyde.This leads to formation of additional hydrogen, shown below.If the band edges of the semiconductor are suitable for oxygen reduction, the oxygen produced by the combination of hydroxyl radicals competes with the H ions for conduction band electrons to produce highly reactive oxidizing species termed as Osuper oxide radicalsO O AA 2 .Because of their strong oxidizing nature, super oxide radicals oxidize methanol back to CO 2 : To evaluate the role of H ions in the reduction of CO 2 , the amount of hydrogen produced in the presence of CO 2 , over TiO 2 , NiO, and ZnO, was measured and plotted in comparison with that of the hydrogen produced in argon environment (without CO 2 ) under similar experimental conditions; see Figs. 345.A substantial decrease in hydrogen evolution was observed for all the catalysts in the presence of CO 2 .This points to the insertion of H ions in CO 2 to give CH 3 OH.The pH changes (Fig. 2) in the course of the photocatalytic process supports the involvement of H ions in multi-electron transfer reduction of CO 2 .

title: Conclusions
content: The use of high photon flux light source such as laser makes it quite feasible to study photocatalytic reactions, such as reduction of CO 2 , in a short time span.Based on our results, it can be concluded that the reduction process does not follow a single mechanism (i.e.reduction of free CO 2 ) but that there are other contributing mecha-nisms such as reduction of H 2 CO 3 and carbonate ions that lead to the same product, i.e. methanol.The maximum yield for photoconversion of CO 2 into methanol over one hour laser irradiation was achieved with NiO; a minimum yield was achieved with TiO 2 .Of the three catalysts, hydrogen yield over TiO 2 was highest while overall production of hydrogen in the presence of CO 2 in the aqueous solution was reduced for all the catalysts. Fig. 1.Comparison of methanol yield produced as a result of photoreduction of CO 2 with 355 nm laser irradiation of 150 mJ over TiO 2 , NiO and ZnO. Fig. 2. Comparison of pH changes under illumination over TiO 2 , NiO and ZnO in the presence of CO 2 and argon. Fig. 3. Comparison of hydrogen production in argon and CO 2 environment over TiO 2 . Fig. 5. Comparison of hydrogen production in argon and CO 2 environment over ZnO. A.H. Yahaya et al. Chemical Physics Letters 400 (2004) 206-212 The support by the Research Institute and Chemistry Department of King Fahd University of Petroleum and Minerals is gratefully acknowledged.Dr. A.H.
